title: Understanding Aphasia After a Stroke
summary: Aphasia — difficulty with language after a stroke — can affect speaking, understanding, reading, or writing, and it's often misunderstood as a thinking problem when it isn't.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Understanding Aphasia After a Stroke | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Aphasia — difficulty with language after a stroke — can affect speaking, understanding, reading, or writing, and it's often misunderstood as a thinking pro
whatYouNeedToKnow: Aphasia affects language, not intelligence — the person's thinking is often intact.
There are different types, affecting speaking and understanding differently.
Speech therapy can lead to real improvement, especially early on.

paragraphs: It's a common and understandable mistake to assume someone with aphasia has lost cognitive ability, when in fact their thinking may be completely intact — the disconnect is specifically in producing or understanding language. This distinction matters enormously for how family members interact with them.

Some people with aphasia struggle mainly to find words while understanding others fine; others have more trouble understanding speech even though their own words come out fluently. A speech-language pathologist can identify the specific pattern and build a therapy plan around it.

nextSteps: Speak in short, simple sentences without raising your voice — hearing usually isn't affected.
Give extra time for responses rather than finishing sentences for them.
Ask about speech-language therapy referrals if not already started.
disclaimer: General information — a speech-language pathologist can offer an assessment specific to the type and severity involved.
